title: A Mortician's Son in Lincoln City
summary: Charles Jarmon opens the round of introductions, describing growing up in an extended family in Lincoln City, Kinston, North Carolina, his father a mortician and his mother a tobacco-factory worker, raised by his grandmother, aunt, and uncle after both parents died before he was twelve, until he left for the military in 1957.

title: The Tuxedo Club and a Country School
summary: Mrs. Stewart recalls her father Isaac Darden, a Kinston businessman who ran a poolroom, a soda shop, and a private club called the Tuxedo Club, her mother the minister of music at First Baptist Church, and being driven by Reverend and Mrs. Moses to a small country school with a potbellied stove until she entered Kinston's public schools as a fourth grader and the new Samson Elementary as a sixth grader.

title: Breakfast, Lunch, and Dinner
summary: John Dudley describes growing up one of eleven children in Mitchell Wooten Court, his father the handyman there and his mother a seasonal tobacco-factory worker who labored alongside German prisoners of war after World War II, and how he put himself through college by working summers in Atlantic City and delivering groceries.

title: Watching His Stories at Lunch
summary: Mrs. Suggs (Frances) remembers a family of five or six on Shine Street, her father a shirt-factory worker who biked home for lunch to watch his stories, her mother working in the tobacco factory, in domestic work, and at Piney Chemical Company, and schooling at Lincoln City Elementary and Tower Hill Road before Adkin.

title: A Schwinn Bike and a Paper Route
summary: Mr. Suggs tells of being an adopted only child from Snow Hill in Greene County, meeting his future wife in the fifth grade, and the Schwinn bike from his biological father that launched him as a boy entrepreneur: a 120-customer paper route, Afro-American subscriptions, Saturday grocery deliveries from the A&P, Sunday shoeshining, and mowing lawns to escape the green-tobacco fields, going to college only to honor his late foster mother.

title: He Gave This to the Boys
summary: Mr. Dove (Jackie) recalls growing up in Mitchell Wooten Court across from Harold, his parents divorcing when he was five, his father handing him fourteen cents to split among the boys, learning to read at four on his mother's lap, lasting one day in kindergarten, his mother taking in laundry and making apple jacks and potato jacks, and his own caddying years at the Kinston country club until he jacked a white golfer up on a fence for calling him boy.

title: I Didn't Know I Was Poor
summary: Dove continues, describing a mother who would not let him play football after he hurt his knees, teaching himself basketball and tennis by watching players like John Dudley and Calvin Thompson, learning to serve with a beat-up racquet, traveling north to Camden and Brooklyn in summers, and going on to play basketball and tennis at Fayetteville State University without ever taking a lesson.

part: Caddies, Croppers, and the Color Line
title: You Knew Where Your Limits Were
summary: Asked about race relations, the men describe caddying at the Kinston country club to stay out of the tobacco field, allowed to play only on Wednesdays at a caddy house run by an African American, earning sixty-five cents for two rounds or more by hunting lost balls, and Dove recounts again the day Larry Beck, who had beaten Jack Nicklaus in the national juniors, called him boy, leading to a fight after which Dove left the club for good.

part: Caddies, Croppers, and the Color Line
title: That Wet Tobacco Slapping in Your Face
summary: Jarmon describes tobacco work as a hand, picked up at dawn, the primers cropping leaves brought by mule-drawn wagon to the barn where women handed and looped the wet tobacco, and Dudley adds the skill of driving the mule-pulled truck between field and barn without overturning it, the croppers passing the time with folklore, jokes, and song as the Golden Leaf was cured.

part: Caddies, Croppers, and the Color Line
title: Nanny, Aren't You Going to Get Up?
topic: Dudley, the Stadiems, and a Bus
summary: After a tobacco-field joke about seeing the Empire State Building, Dudley recounts working for H. Stadiem, a Jewish merchant ostracized for marrying a white woman, who let him drive and found him lodging on sales trips, and a bus incident when he was nine, when a white man told his mother to give up her seat and the bus driver, overhearing, told the man to sit elsewhere among the many empty seats.

part: Caddies, Croppers, and the Color Line
title: And So Is Toilet Paper
topic: Dove's Bus and the Hannibal Suit
summary: Dove tells of a bus ride at twelve when a white woman demanded his seat saying I'm white and he replied and so is toilet paper, the driver moving him to the rear until black men there said leave that boy alone, and the group recalls that within a year Dr. Hannibal's wife, an activist, challenged the bus company's license before the ICC over segregated seating, after which Kinston's bus system disappeared.

part: Caddies, Croppers, and the Color Line
title: We Don't Want Any of It
summary: Mrs. Stewart recounts getting tobacco poison handing in the barn and seeing Dr. Hannibal, then trying cotton-picking for one day, and working years as a waitress at Standard Drug Store where Afro-Americans could not sit but ordered at the end of the counter, and a five-and-dime called Rose's where a group of them ordered armfuls of candy then said we don't want any of it and walked out.

part: Caddies, Croppers, and the Color Line
title: Camaraderie in the Field
summary: The group weighs working tobacco among a larger group against the isolation of caddying, agreeing the field was integrated with white croppers, even the farmer or his son, sharing a mason jar of water and a degree of camaraderie absent outside it, the owner's wife preparing lunch eaten at the table, and detailing the wage structure of primers at a dollar an hour against barn workers at four dollars a day.

part: Caddies, Croppers, and the Color Line
title: A Spread Just Like the Ponderosa
summary: They describe where croppers stayed, sleeping under the barn and cooking corn in the ashes, nipping the suckers off growing plants, the curing process, and the end-of-season barbecue with pigs and watermelon that made you want to stay with a farmer, the radio station WFTC (World's Foremost Tobacco Center), and the North Carolina State Wolfpack golfers who took over the Kinston club on Wednesdays.

part: Caddies, Croppers, and the Color Line
title: K-Town and the Chitlin' Circuit
summary: The Beaches sponsored Chitlin' Circuit performers like James Brown, Ruth Brown, and Arthur Prysock, and Mr. Suggs describes Kinston as K-Town, a hub among Camp Lejeune, Cherry Point, Fort Bragg, and Seymour Johnson, where he scalped dance tickets bought at a dollar fifty and sold at two dollars, where the young men had to defend their sisters from servicemen, and weekend accidents killed liquored-up soldiers heading back to base, a scene that lasted into the fifties.

part: The Buses and the Drug Store
title: The Line of Demarcation
summary: Returning to the buses, the group explains there were no separate white and black lines but a single transit system where whites sat up front and blacks in the back, the line of demarcation at the side back door, blacks forced to move back as the bus filled, and Dove and Mr. Suggs recount being told to shut a child up and the white prerogative that let a man sit beside Dove while a woman behind him demanded only the black boy's seat.

part: The Buses and the Drug Store
title: All He Needed to Say Was Mgawa
summary: Mr. Suggs describes a scheme two weeks after the Greensboro sit-ins in February 1960 to integrate Standard Drug Number Two: dressing his friend Curtis Henderson as a newly free Ghanaian diplomat who spoke only Tarzan's Mgawa while Suggs interpreted in French, convincing a confused waitress and a young white manager who feared a diplomatic problem, a friend from the projects refusing to serve them before the food came, and the three of them running back to East Kinston after the meal.

part: The Buses and the Drug Store
title: Little Things We Could Get Away With
summary: The group shares quiet acts of defiance: a sister who dropped a prescription after a long wait, tasting the white water fountain to find the difference, and Mr. Suggs and his brother stealing Budweiser from supply cars by the Kinston Indians stadium railroad track to cool in the stream behind Mitchell Wooten Court, escaping the court case that caught the other boys.

part: The Buses and the Drug Store
title: They Threw Rocks Every Saturday
summary: Asked about interactions with whites, Mr. Suggs describes living beside the poor whites of the Simon Bright apartments, swimming with a boy named Bo and shining shoes to the bus station on Sundays, while Jarmon recalls the white kids who lay in wait throwing rocks as he walked past the white theater to the black one, and Mrs. Suggs tells of white boys who knocked her off her bicycle, took it, and spat on her.

title: Is There a School in Our Area Like This?
summary: Dudley, of the class of 1952, explains the walkout began in a Wednesday civics class with the motivational teacher B.C. Davis, when the weekly reader's topic was what an ideal school has, and the students asked whether such a school existed nearby; told it was Grainger High up on the hill, they asked Mrs. Davis to leave the room so they could discuss it without involving her.

title: Petition the School Board
summary: The students drew up eight items they needed, kept their teacher out so she could not be blamed, and won the principal Stewart's permission to meet without teachers in the auditorium and then the old barn, where, trusted as responsible kids, they petitioned the school board for a Monday hearing and resolved that if the board would not respond they would walk out.

title: Carolyn Has Lost Her Red Pocketbook
topic: The Board Says No and the Signal
summary: With all 720 students sworn to tell no parent or teacher and a coded signal that Carolyn had lost a red pocketbook, a committee of five met the board, who demanded who sent you and said the request was not in the budget for ten years; the next morning Dudley made the announcement and 720 students rose orderly, picked up pre-made placards, and marched down Queen Street.

title: So Be It, We'll Take Our Chances
summary: Still gathered around the school to strategize, the committee was warned by the board that seniors would not benefit and would not graduate if any of the 720 students caused trouble, and answered that a quality education mattered for their brothers and sisters; the students stayed out until the Monday after Thanksgiving, congregating at the Carver Theater, with Dr. Hannibal's activist wife as a next-door neighbor.

title: What Am I Going to Say to My Parents?
summary: Others describe their reactions, fear of facing their parents and going along with the crowd, parents who proved kind and asked only whether they had thought it through, and the deeper reason the students kept silent: their parents and teachers could lose their jobs, so even the principal's son, Mrs. Stewart's late husband, walked out without telling his father.

title: There Was No Talking, We Just Rose Up
summary: Participants recall the eerie quiet as 720 students rose when the announcement was made and walked out, the Quality Education placards hidden off campus and gathered at the Carver Theater, teachers amazed, and a seventh-grade girl whose religious teacher barred the classroom door so her class never joined the march but waited at the flagpole, the five leaders expressing no fear because the action was simply needed.

title: Ten Years Became Eighteen Months
summary: Dudley reflects that the significance struck him only at the 2010 reenactment, that the eight petitioned items were built in eighteen months rather than ten years, and explains the committee of five decided to bring the 720 students back the Monday after Thanksgiving by calling designated key people once they felt they had made their point.

title: Leave Those Kids Alone
summary: Dudley describes six seniors guarding Dr. Alice Hannibal's house for a week after bomb threats over her letters, no threats against the students' own families, and the Free Press editor Jake Strother probing Dove's laundress mother, who knew nothing; the group credits the principal Stewart, who told the panicked teachers to leave those kids alone, and other teachers who quietly let them meet without adults.

title: A State-of-the-Art Gym and the Brown Backdrop
topic: The Result and the Legal Context
summary: They recount winning everything they asked for, a state-of-the-art gymnasium, home economics suite, band room, and classrooms, set the November 1951 walkout against the backdrop of Brown v. Board then before the Supreme Court, and note the parallel student cases, including Barbara Johns's Prince Edward County walkout at Moton High in 1951, which they had not known about when they organized.
related_events: Davis v. County School Board of Prince Edward County

part: The Gym, the Championship, and Adkin's Legacy
title: Halloween Helmets and a State Title
summary: The group recalls the hand-me-down athletic equipment from Grainger High, brought by a recreation worker named Busby, the Halloween-lantern helmets they wore, and how, despite it, the 1952-53 Adkin team won the North Carolina state championship, beating the Little Blues of Raleigh and Dudley High, forced to play the title game at Grainger's stadium because they had none of their own.

part: The Gym, the Championship, and Adkin's Legacy
title: The Gym the Students Built
topic: Recognition, the Gym, and Althea Gibson
summary: They describe holding fish fries to buy their own championship jackets with no community receptions, the tiny gymnasium built by students in vocational class with a potbellied stove and no seats, an incident when a pin stuck in an opposing player was wrongly blamed on Dove until B.C. Davis intervened, and the great Althea Gibson of Williston High playing in that gym and beating their team almost by herself.

part: The Gym, the Championship, and Adkin's Legacy
title: And the Gym Burned Down
summary: The group recalls football helmets thin as tams and how the student-built gym burned down after ashes from a warming fire caught the floor while they played, which got them the new state-of-the-art facility, and that the school itself closed with desegregation in 1970, recounting the indignity of having no mail delivered, so Dudley biked to the Queen Street post office for the school's mail in exchange for a free lunch.

part: The Gym, the Championship, and Adkin's Legacy
title: A Creek That Shared the School's Name
summary: They describe the creek beside the campus that flooded the Mitchell Wooten Court apartments and drowned a first cousin, the only swimming they got, and identify Adkin High as a Rosenwald school, now gone with its original building, the gym the only survivor, and explain Rosenwald schools were built across the South for black children by the Sears head Julius Rosenwald at Booker T. Washington's urging, requiring community money and industrial courses.

part: The Gym, the Championship, and Adkin's Legacy
title: A Doctor's Wife Who Could Not Be Touched
summary: The group portrays Alice Hannibal, born in New Jersey with a Jamaican husband trained at Fisk, the first black person on Kinston's city council, in whose yard the Klan burned crosses, who could act for education because her husband's medical practice made the family independent; her children went on to be doctors and a dentist, one working under Floyd McKissick at Soul City, and Dove recalls her bathing his back after he was shot on prom night, with a municipal building now named for her.

title: If Mr. Charlie Said Do This
summary: Asked about their parents' influence, the group reflects that class reunions keep the walkout alive, that most of their parents had not finished high school and could not grasp its impact in a world where one did what Mr. Charlie said, and Mr. Suggs recalls black adults on Federal Street in Durham telling student marchers under Floyd McKissick and CORE to go back to school, a generational gulf he imagines was wider still in 1952.

title: I Never Looked at Myself as a Leader
summary: Dudley reflects that his high-school leadership followed him into college as class and student-body president though he credited it to innate drive and dedicated teachers rather than his parents, and Mrs. Stewart describes how she and her late husband Reggie, longtime alumni-association president, reconstructed the walkout story from a letter by committee member Frederick Thompson to post in the gymnasium.

title: You Never Told Me How We Got This Gym
summary: Mrs. Stewart proposes a PowerPoint presentation for the alumni banquet so the story reaches members who cannot hear it over the gym's poor sound system, recalling a niece who never knew how they got the gymnasium because the cafeteria-head mother had stayed silent, and the group agrees they appreciate the walkout more every year; they then explain the application for National Register of Historic Places recognition of the gym was withdrawn because the original school is gone and twenty-three apartments now modify the structure, while the editor who penned the 2010 story won national editor of the year and Mrs. Stewart's husband commissioned an artist's drawing to keep the vanished school alive.

title: Intelligence for the Freedom Rides
summary: Mr. Suggs, who brought the walkout to the Smithsonian, describes the interview as his own step into the Civil Rights Movement, recounting his first actions against Jim Crow on the bus and at a Kinston lunch counter, joining CORE after a December 1960 ride James Farmer assigned him to chart where black travelers could stop between Baltimore and Washington, work that turned out to be intelligence for the Freedom Rides, then organizing campus to campus as a college athlete and sitting in at Fayetteville State.

title: The Blood Is on Your Hands
summary: Mr. Suggs recounts a Fayetteville sit-in where a police sergeant harassed him over his draft card, white soldiers from Fort Bragg angrily telling the officer off, his being taken into custody at Pope Air Force Base's FBI office where he warned the agent the blood would be on his hands before producing the card, and the police chief's parting threat that if he ever got him in jail he might not come out alive.

title: I Was the Guy Who Wrote the Position Paper
summary: After an injunction bearing his name, Mr. Suggs left Fayetteville State for New York but kept organizing in every southern state except Mississippi, planning to retire at fifty-five after seeing too many colleagues die young, and describes his role in SNCC until it ended as writing position papers by assignment for James Forman, sometimes funded by Harry Belafonte, and later chairing the D.C. Coalition for Youth that built Marion Barry's summer youth program.

title: Keep Them Thinking on a Larger Plane
summary: At Mr. Suggs's urging, Mrs. Stewart (Eleanor) describes the boys' choir she formed before retiring from D.C. public schools in 1993, now marking its twentieth anniversary with tours to Austria, Switzerland, Paris, England, and China and an invitation to Italy, her passion to open boys' eyes to a larger world, with a partnership with the Morehouse glee club, before the interview closes with thanks and a note that John Bishop filmed and Elaine Nichols of the Smithsonian took part.
